1:
"For this cause I Paul, the prisoner of Jesus Christ for you Gentiles," The witnesses note Paul's describing himself as the prisoner of Christ for the Gentiles. Gill notes Paul was a prisoner not for any capital crime, but for Christ, on account of preaching Christ and his gospel; so he was not ashamed of his bonds but gloried in them, a prison often being the very port of the gospel's advance. The witnesses note Paul, beginning a prayer (resumed at v.14), describing himself as the prisoner of Jesus Christ for the sake of the Gentiles; the honoured bondage borne for the Gentiles — that Paul, far from being ashamed of his imprisonment, names himself "the prisoner of Jesus Christ," counting his chains an honour, for he was bound not as a criminal but for the very cause of bringing the gospel to the Gentiles, so that his bonds were the badge of his faithful service to them; the prisoner of Jesus Christ for you Gentiles. The witnesses note I Paul, the prisoner of Jesus Christ. The witnesses note for you Gentiles.

The witnesses note Paul calling himself "the prisoner of Jesus Christ for you Gentiles" — his chains an honour, the badge of his faithful service to them.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's honoured bondage for the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul counted his chains an honour, bound for the cause of the gospel to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the prisoner: "I Paul, the prisoner of Jesus Christ" — Gill notes Paul was a prisoner for Christ, not for any crime; the prisoner; the of Jesus Christ; the for Christ; the not a crime; the bonds. The expositors observe the not ashamed: Paul was not ashamed of his bonds but gloried in them; the not ashamed; the gloried; the bonds; the honour; the badge. The witnesses press the for the Gentiles: "for you Gentiles" — Paul was bound for the sake of the Gentiles; the for the Gentiles; the for you; the their sake; the the cause; the service. The expositors draw the lesson on the honoured bondage: Paul names himself the prisoner of Christ, counting his chains an honour; the honoured bondage; the prisoner of Christ; the chains; the honour; the gloried. The witnesses observe the for the gospel: he was bound for the very cause of bringing the gospel to the Gentiles; the for the gospel; the cause; the bringing; the Gentiles; the service. The expositors note the badge of service: his bonds were the badge of his faithful service; the badge; the bonds; the faithful service; the honour; the Gentiles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I Paul the prisoner of Jesus Christ, the for you Gentiles, and the honoured bondage. The verse thus records Paul's honoured bondage for the Gentiles — teaching that he counted his chains an honour, bound for the cause of the gospel to the Gentiles.
2:
"If ye have heard of the dispensation of the grace of God which is given me to you-ward:" The witnesses note the stewardship of grace committed to Paul for the Gentiles. Gill notes the "dispensation of the grace of God" is the doctrine of grace, the gospel, the subject matter of his ministry, committed to him as a trust for them. The witnesses note Paul referring to the dispensation of the grace of God given him toward the Gentiles, which they had heard of; the stewardship of the gospel for the Gentiles — that Paul speaks of the special trust ("dispensation," stewardship) of God's grace committed to him: the office and commission to dispense the gospel of grace to the Gentiles, a charge of which they had heard, so that his apostleship to them was no self-chosen role but a divine stewardship of grace laid upon him for their good; the dispensation of the grace of God. The witnesses note the dispensation of the grace of God. The witnesses note which is given me to you-ward.

The witnesses note the stewardship of grace committed to Paul for the Gentiles — the gospel of grace a divine trust laid on him to dispense for their good.

So the witnesses make the verse the stewardship of grace for the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine trust of the gospel committed to Paul to dispense to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the dispensation: "the dispensation of the grace of God" — Gill notes the doctrine of grace, the gospel, committed to Paul; the dispensation; the grace of God; the gospel; the doctrine; the trust. The expositors observe the stewardship: the dispensation is the stewardship, the office of dispensing the gospel; the stewardship; the office; the dispensing; the the gospel; the commission. The witnesses press the given to Paul: "which is given me" — the grace given to Paul; the given; the to Paul; the committed; the trust; the office. The expositors draw the lesson on the trust for the Gentiles: "to you-ward" — the trust was toward the Gentiles, for their good; the trust; the toward the Gentiles; the for their good; the to you-ward; the charge. The witnesses observe the divine stewardship: Paul's apostleship was a divine stewardship of grace, not self-chosen; the divine stewardship; the not self-chosen; the apostleship; the grace; the laid on him. The expositors note the heard of: the Gentiles had heard of this dispensation; the heard of; the the Gentiles; the known; the the dispensation; the reported. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the dispensation of the grace of God, the which is given me to you-ward, and the stewardship of grace. The verse thus records the stewardship of grace for the Gentiles — teaching the divine trust of the gospel committed to Paul to dispense to the Gentiles.
3:
"How that by revelation he made known unto me the mystery; (as I wrote afore in few words," The witnesses note that the mystery was made known to Paul by revelation. Gill notes the gospel is sometimes called a mystery — the mystery of the gospel, of godliness, of faith — its several doctrines being the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, such as the Trinity and the like. The witnesses note Paul declaring that by revelation God made known to him the mystery, as he had briefly written before; the mystery received by divine revelation — that Paul received the gospel-mystery (chiefly the truth of the Gentiles' inclusion in Christ) not from men nor by study, but by direct revelation from God, who unveiled to him what had been hidden, so that his message rested on God's own disclosure, not human invention; by revelation he made known unto me the mystery. The witnesses note by revelation he made known unto me the mystery. The witnesses note as I wrote afore in few words.

The witnesses note the mystery made known to Paul by revelation — received not from men nor by study, but by God's own unveiling of what had been hidden.

So the witnesses make the verse the mystery received by revelation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul received the gospel-mystery by direct divine revelation, not human invention. The witnesses note the revelation: "by revelation he made known unto me the mystery" — Gill notes the gospel-mystery was made known by revelation; the revelation; the made known; the mystery; the by God; the unveiled. The expositors observe the mystery: the mystery is the gospel, chiefly the Gentiles' inclusion in Christ; the mystery; the the gospel; the Gentiles' inclusion; the hidden; the disclosed. The witnesses press the by revelation: Paul received it not from men nor by study, but by direct revelation; the by revelation; the not from men; the not by study; the direct; the God. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine disclosure: God unveiled to Paul what had been hidden; the divine disclosure; the unveiled; the hidden; the made known; the God's. The witnesses observe the rests on God: Paul's message rested on God's own disclosure, not human invention; the rests on God; the disclosure; the not invention; the God's; the divine. The expositors note the wrote afore: "as I wrote afore in few words" — Paul had briefly written of it before; the wrote afore; the briefly; the before; the few words; the written. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the by revelation he made known unto me the mystery, the as I wrote afore in few words, and the mystery by revelation. The verse thus records the mystery received by revelation — teaching that Paul received the gospel-mystery by direct divine revelation, not human invention.
4:
"Whereby, when ye read, ye may understand my knowledge in the mystery of Christ)" The witnesses note that Paul's writing reveals his understanding of the mystery. Gill notes that by reading the foregoing chapters and seriously considering what is contained in them, they may perceive Paul's knowledge. Barnes notes that by the bare reading they may understand the view he entertains of the plan of salvation. The witnesses note Paul observing that, when they read what he has written, they may understand his knowledge in the mystery of Christ; the apostle's insight open to the reader — that Paul, far from withholding his understanding, has set down in writing such things that any who read with care will perceive the depth of his insight into "the mystery of Christ" (the whole gospel-secret of redemption and the Gentiles' calling), so that his readers are made sharers in the revelation given him; my knowledge in the mystery of Christ. The witnesses note when ye read, ye may understand. The witnesses note my knowledge in the mystery of Christ.

The witnesses note that Paul's writing opens his understanding to the reader — any who read with care perceive his insight into the mystery of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostle's insight open to the reader. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's writing makes his readers sharers in the revelation of the mystery of Christ. The witnesses note the reading: "when ye read" — Gill notes by reading the foregoing chapters; the reading; the read; the the chapters; the consider; the perceive. The expositors observe the understanding: "ye may understand my knowledge" — they may understand Paul's knowledge; the understanding; the my knowledge; the perceive; the insight; the grasp. The witnesses press the mystery of Christ: "in the mystery of Christ" — Paul's knowledge in the mystery of Christ; the mystery of Christ; the the gospel-secret; the redemption; the Gentiles' calling; the insight. The expositors draw the lesson on the insight open: Paul has set his understanding in writing, open to the careful reader; the insight open; the in writing; the careful reader; the perceive; the depth. The witnesses observe the sharers in revelation: the readers are made sharers in the revelation given Paul; the sharers; the revelation; the given Paul; the readers; the made. The expositors note the not withholding: Paul does not withhold his understanding, but sets it down; the not withholding; the sets down; the open; the writing; the shares. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when ye read ye may understand, the my knowledge in the mystery of Christ, and the insight open. The verse thus records the apostle's insight open to the reader — teaching that Paul's writing makes his readers sharers in the revelation of the mystery of Christ.
5:
"Which in other ages was not made known unto the sons of men, as it is now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit;" The witnesses note the fuller revelation of the mystery now given. Gill notes the mystery was not made known to men in general, nor so clearly, under former ages — though some hints were given to Adam after the fall, the gospel preached before to Abraham, and Moses and David knew something of it — but it is now more fully revealed. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the mystery, not made known in former ages as it now is, has been revealed to the holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit; the new clearness of the gospel revelation — that the truth now made plain (the Gentiles' full and equal sharing in Christ) was, in former ages, hidden or only dimly intimated, but is now clearly unveiled by the Spirit to the New Testament apostles and prophets, the bearers of the completed revelation, so that what the saints of old saw afar off is now openly displayed; now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets. The witnesses note in other ages was not made known. The witnesses note now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit.

The witnesses note the mystery, hidden or dimly seen in former ages, now revealed clearly to the apostles and prophets by the Spirit — the new clearness of the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the fuller revelation now given. The lesson the expositors draw is the new clearness of the gospel, the truth dimly seen of old now openly unveiled by the Spirit. The witnesses note the former ages: "which in other ages was not made known unto the sons of men" — Gill notes the mystery was not made known so clearly in former ages; the former ages; the not made known; the hidden; the dimly; the of old. The expositors observe the hints of old: some hints were given to Adam, Abraham, Moses, and David; the hints; the Adam; the Abraham; the Moses and David; the dimly. The witnesses press the now revealed: "as it is now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets" — now revealed to the apostles and prophets; the now revealed; the apostles; the prophets; the New Testament; the unveiled. The expositors draw the lesson on the new clearness: the truth now made plain (the Gentiles' full sharing in Christ) was once hidden; the new clearness; the made plain; the Gentiles' sharing; the once hidden; the unveiled. The witnesses observe the by the Spirit: "by the Spirit" — the revelation came by the Spirit; the by the Spirit; the revelation; the the Spirit; the came; the unveiled. The expositors note the completed revelation: the apostles and prophets are the bearers of the completed revelation; the completed revelation; the apostles and prophets; the bearers; the openly; the displayed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in other ages was not made known, the now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit, and the new clearness. The verse thus records the fuller revelation now given — teaching the new clearness of the gospel, the truth dimly seen of old now openly unveiled by the Spirit.
6:
"That the Gentiles should be fellowheirs, and of the same body, and partakers of his promise in Christ by the gospel:" The witnesses note the substance of the mystery — the Gentiles made one with the Jews in Christ. Gill notes the Gentiles should be fellowheirs with the Jews of all the blessings of grace and the eternal inheritance; heirs they are by being Christ's, by adoption, and by justification through his righteousness. The witnesses note Paul stating the very content of the mystery — that the Gentiles should be fellowheirs, of the same body, and partakers of God's promise in Christ through the gospel; the full equality of the Gentiles in Christ — that the great secret now revealed is the Gentiles' complete and equal share with the Jews in the privileges of grace: joint heirs of the same inheritance, members of the same body (the church), and partakers of the same promise, all in Christ and through the gospel, the old wall between Jew and Gentile wholly abolished; fellowheirs, of the same body, partakers of his promise. The witnesses note the Gentiles should be fellowheirs, and of the same body. The witnesses note partakers of his promise in Christ by the gospel.

The witnesses note the substance of the mystery — the Gentiles made fellowheirs, of the same body, partakers of the promise — their full equality with the Jews in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the substance of the mystery. The lesson the expositors draw is the Gentiles' complete and equal share with the Jews in the privileges of grace, all in Christ. The witnesses note the fellowheirs: "the Gentiles should be fellowheirs" — Gill notes the Gentiles are fellowheirs with the Jews of all the blessings; the fellowheirs; the with the Jews; the the blessings; the inheritance; the joint heirs. The expositors observe the same body: "and of the same body" — members of the same body (the church); the same body; the the church; the members; the one body; the united. The witnesses press the partakers of the promise: "and partakers of his promise in Christ by the gospel" — partakers of God's promise in Christ through the gospel; the partakers; the the promise; the in Christ; the the gospel; the share. The expositors draw the lesson on the full equality: the great secret is the Gentiles' complete and equal share with the Jews; the full equality; the complete; the equal share; the with the Jews; the grace. The witnesses observe the wall abolished: the old wall between Jew and Gentile is wholly abolished; the wall abolished; the old wall; the Jew and Gentile; the abolished; the united. The expositors note the all in Christ: all this is in Christ and through the gospel; the all in Christ; the in Christ; the through the gospel; the the channel; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the Gentiles should be fellowheirs and of the same body, the partakers of his promise in Christ by the gospel, and the substance of the mystery. The verse thus records the substance of the mystery — teaching the Gentiles' complete and equal share with the Jews in the privileges of grace, all in Christ.
7:
"Whereof I was made a minister, according to the gift of the grace of God given unto me by the effectual working of his power." The witnesses note Paul's ministry as the gift of God's grace and power. Gill notes Paul was made a minister of the gospel not by men but by God; he is a true minister who is called of God to the work, qualified by him with grace and gifts, and faithfully discharges it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he was made a minister of this gospel according to the gift of God's grace, given by the effectual working of his power; the ministry as a gift of grace and power — that Paul ascribes his whole ministry to God: he became a minister not by his own choosing or worth, but by the free gift of God's grace, and was made fit and effective for it by the mighty, effectual working of God's power, so that both his office and his ability to fill it were wholly of God; made a minister... by the effectual working of his power. The witnesses note whereof I was made a minister. The witnesses note by the effectual working of his power.

The witnesses note Paul's ministry as the gift of God's grace and power — his office and his ability to fill it both wholly of God, not his own worth.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's ministry as a gift of grace and power. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul ascribed his whole ministry — office and ability — to God's free grace and effectual power. The witnesses note the made a minister: "whereof I was made a minister" — Gill notes Paul was made a minister by God, not men; the made a minister; the by God; the not men; the called; the office. The expositors observe the gift of grace: "according to the gift of the grace of God given unto me" — by the free gift of God's grace; the gift of grace; the free gift; the given; the not his worth; the grace. The witnesses press the effectual power: "by the effectual working of his power" — made fit and effective by the mighty working of God's power; the effectual power; the mighty working; the made fit; the effective; the God's power. The expositors draw the lesson on the office of God: Paul became a minister not by his own choosing or worth, but by God's gift; the office of God; the not his choosing; the not his worth; the God's gift; the grace. The witnesses observe the ability of God: his ability to fill the office was by God's effectual power; the ability of God; the to fill; the God's power; the effectual; the made fit. The expositors note the wholly of God: both his office and his ability were wholly of God; the wholly of God; the office; the ability; the both; the God's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whereof I was made a minister, the by the effectual working of his power, and the ministry as a gift. The verse thus records Paul's ministry as a gift of grace and power — teaching that he ascribed his whole ministry to God's free grace and effectual power.
8:
"Unto me, who am less than the least of all saints, is this grace given, that I should preach among the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ;" The witnesses note Paul's deep humility joined to his high commission. Chrysostom notes the surpassing lowliness of Paul, who, when about to speak of the vastness of God's grace, calls himself "less than the least of all saints" — coining a word beyond grammar to express his sense of his own unworthiness. The witnesses note Paul declaring that to him, less than the least of all saints, this grace was given, to preach among the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ; the unworthy preacher of inexhaustible riches — that Paul, with the deepest self-abasement (counting himself below the lowest of the saints, remembering his former persecutions), marvels that so high a grace was given to one so unworthy: the commission to proclaim to the Gentiles the "unsearchable riches of Christ," treasures of grace and glory so vast they can never be traced out or exhausted; the unsearchable riches of Christ. The witnesses note less than the least of all saints. The witnesses note the unsearchable riches of Christ.

The witnesses note Paul's humility and high commission — "less than the least of all saints," yet given to preach "the unsearchable riches of Christ" to the Gentiles.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's deep humility joined to his high commission. The lesson the expositors draw is the unworthy preacher entrusted with the inexhaustible riches of Christ. The witnesses note the lowliness: "unto me, who am less than the least of all saints" — Chrysostom notes Paul's surpassing lowliness, coining a word beyond grammar; the lowliness; the less than the least; the self-abasement; the unworthy; the humble. The expositors observe the grace given: "is this grace given" — the high grace given to one so unworthy; the grace given; the high; the to the unworthy; the marvels; the gift. The witnesses press the preaching: "that I should preach among the Gentiles" — that he should preach to the Gentiles; the preaching; the among the Gentiles; the commission; the proclaim; the office. The expositors draw the lesson on the unsearchable riches: "the unsearchable riches of Christ" — the riches of Christ that can never be traced out or exhausted; the unsearchable riches; the never traced; the inexhaustible; the of Christ; the treasures. The witnesses observe the self-abasement: Paul counts himself below the lowest, remembering his former persecutions; the self-abasement; the below the lowest; the persecutions; the unworthy; the humble. The expositors note the marvel: Paul marvels that so high a grace was given to one so unworthy; the marvel; the so high; the grace; the unworthy; the wonder. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the less than the least of all saints, the the unsearchable riches of Christ, and the humility and commission. The verse thus records Paul's deep humility joined to his high commission — teaching the unworthy preacher entrusted with the inexhaustible riches of Christ.
9:
"And to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery, which from the beginning of the world hath been hid in God, who created all things by Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note Paul's commission to make plain the long-hidden mystery. Gill notes the "fellowship of the mystery" (or the dispensation of it) is the communication of grace by the gospel, a participation of its truths and doctrines, and the communion with Father, Son, and Spirit to which it leads. The witnesses note Paul declaring his charge to make all men see the fellowship of the mystery, which from the world's beginning had been hidden in God, the Creator of all things by Jesus Christ; the open publication of what was hidden in God — that Paul's task was to bring into the light, for all to see, the gospel-mystery that had lain concealed in God's own eternal counsel from the foundation of the world, now at last unveiled, the same God who had hidden it being the Creator of all things by Christ; hid in God, who created all things by Jesus Christ. The witnesses note to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery. The witnesses note hid in God, who created all things by Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note Paul's charge to make plain the mystery hidden from the world's beginning in God — now brought into the light for all to see.

So the witnesses make the verse the open publication of the long-hidden mystery. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's task was to unveil for all the gospel-mystery once concealed in God's eternal counsel. The witnesses note the making see: "to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery" — Gill notes the fellowship of the mystery is the communication of grace by the gospel; the making see; the all men; the the fellowship; the communication; the unveil. The expositors observe the fellowship: the fellowship is the participation in the gospel's truths and the communion with God; the fellowship; the participation; the truths; the communion; the with God. The witnesses press the hidden: "which from the beginning of the world hath been hid in God" — hidden in God from the world's beginning; the hidden; the from the beginning; the in God; the concealed; the eternal counsel. The expositors draw the lesson on the open publication: Paul's task was to bring into the light, for all to see, what had been concealed; the open publication; the into the light; the for all; the concealed; the unveil. The witnesses observe the in God's counsel: the mystery lay concealed in God's own eternal counsel; the in God's counsel; the eternal; the concealed; the God's own; the hidden. The expositors note the Creator: "who created all things by Jesus Christ" — the same God who hid it is the Creator of all by Christ; the Creator; the all things; the by Christ; the God; the made. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery, the hid in God who created all things by Jesus Christ, and the open publication. The verse thus records the open publication of the long-hidden mystery — teaching that Paul's task was to unveil for all the gospel-mystery once concealed in God's eternal counsel.
10:
"To the intent that now, unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places, might be known by the church the manifold wisdom of God," The witnesses note that the angels learn God's manifold wisdom through the church. Gill notes that by "principalities and powers in heavenly places" are meant, not civil magistrates nor evil angels, but the good angels, the angels in heaven. The witnesses note Paul declaring the further purpose — that now, through the church, the manifold wisdom of God might be made known to the principalities and powers in heavenly places; the church as the theatre of God's wisdom to angels — that God's grand design in the gospel-mystery reaches even to the angelic world: the holy angels, beholding the church (Jew and Gentile gathered into one body by grace), learn and admire the "manifold" (many-coloured, richly varied) wisdom of God, which devised so wonderful a salvation, the redeemed company being the very display before heaven of God's wisdom; the manifold wisdom of God. The witnesses note unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places. The witnesses note might be known by the church the manifold wisdom of God.

The witnesses note the angels learning God's manifold wisdom through the church — the redeemed company the display before heaven of God's richly varied wisdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the church as the theatre of God's wisdom to angels. The lesson the expositors draw is that the holy angels learn and admire God's manifold wisdom by beholding the church. The witnesses note the principalities: "unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places" — Gill notes the good angels in heaven; the principalities; the powers; the good angels; the in heaven; the heavenly. The expositors observe the made known: "might be known by the church" — God's wisdom made known through the church; the made known; the by the church; the through; the display; the taught. The witnesses press the manifold wisdom: "the manifold wisdom of God" — the manifold (many-coloured, richly varied) wisdom of God; the manifold wisdom; the many-coloured; the varied; the rich; the of God. The expositors draw the lesson on the angels learning: the holy angels, beholding the church, learn and admire God's wisdom; the angels learning; the beholding; the the church; the admire; the wisdom. The witnesses observe the church the display: the redeemed company (Jew and Gentile in one body) is the display before heaven of God's wisdom; the church the display; the Jew and Gentile; the one body; the before heaven; the wisdom. The expositors note the wonderful salvation: God's wisdom devised so wonderful a salvation, which the angels admire; the wonderful salvation; the devised; the the angels; the admire; the wisdom. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places, the might be known by the church the manifold wisdom of God, and the church the theatre of wisdom. The verse thus records the church as the theatre of God's wisdom to angels — teaching that the holy angels learn and admire God's manifold wisdom by beholding the church.
11:
"According to the eternal purpose which he purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord:" The witnesses note that all rests on God's eternal purpose in Christ. Gill notes the whole of salvation, displaying God's great wisdom, is according to a purpose of his — the scheme fixed in the council of peace, the thing effected in pursuance of it, Christ set forth in it, his incarnation, sufferings, and death all decreed by God. The witnesses note Paul declaring that all this is according to the eternal purpose which God purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord; the grounding of the whole plan in God's eternal counsel — that the entire mystery (the Gentiles' calling, the church, the display of wisdom to angels) was no afterthought or accident of history, but the unfolding in time of a purpose God had formed in eternity and centred in Christ, so that the gospel rests on the firm foundation of God's everlasting design; the eternal purpose which he purposed in Christ Jesus. The witnesses note according to the eternal purpose. The witnesses note which he purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord.

The witnesses note that all rests on God's eternal purpose in Christ — the whole mystery the unfolding of a design God formed in eternity and centred in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the grounding of the plan in God's eternal purpose. The lesson the expositors draw is that the whole mystery is the unfolding of a purpose God formed in eternity, centred in Christ. The witnesses note the eternal purpose: "according to the eternal purpose" — Gill notes the whole of salvation is according to God's purpose; the eternal purpose; the God's purpose; the the scheme; the council of peace; the design. The expositors observe the purposed in Christ: "which he purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord" — purposed in Christ; the purposed; the in Christ; the centred; the the Lord; the design. The witnesses press the no afterthought: the mystery was no afterthought or accident of history; the no afterthought; the not accident; the not chance; the the mystery; the purposed. The expositors draw the lesson on the unfolding in time: the mystery is the unfolding in time of a purpose formed in eternity; the unfolding; the in time; the formed in eternity; the purpose; the design. The witnesses observe the centred in Christ: the eternal purpose is centred in Christ; the centred in Christ; the in Christ; the the Lord; the focus; the design. The expositors note the firm foundation: the gospel rests on the firm foundation of God's everlasting design; the firm foundation; the everlasting design; the rests; the gospel; the sure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the according to the eternal purpose, the which he purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord, and the eternal purpose. The verse thus records the grounding of the plan in God's eternal purpose — teaching that the whole mystery is the unfolding of a purpose God formed in eternity, centred in Christ.
12:
"In whom we have boldness and access with confidence by the faith of him." The witnesses note the believer's bold access to God in Christ. Gill notes that in Christ we have access into the holy of holies, to the throne of grace and to God the Father; Christ is the way of access, union to him giving the right, and his mediation procuring audience and acceptance. The witnesses note Paul declaring that in Christ believers have boldness and access to God with confidence through faith in him; the free and confident approach to God through Christ — that this same Christ, in whom God's eternal purpose centres, is the one through whom believers draw near to God: not timidly or as strangers, but with boldness (freedom of speech), access (an introduction into the very presence of the Father), and confidence (settled assurance), all received by faith in Christ, whose mediation opens the way; boldness and access with confidence. The witnesses note boldness and access with confidence. The witnesses note by the faith of him.

The witnesses note the believer's bold access to God in Christ — drawing near not as strangers but with boldness, access, and confidence, through faith in him.

So the witnesses make the verse the believer's confident access in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the free and confident approach to God through Christ, received by faith. The witnesses note the in whom: "in whom we have boldness and access" — Gill notes in Christ we have access to God; the in whom; the in Christ; the access; the to God; the the way. The expositors observe the boldness: "boldness" — boldness (freedom of speech) before God; the boldness; the freedom; the of speech; the before God; the free. The witnesses press the access: "and access" — access (an introduction into the Father's presence); the access; the introduction; the the presence; the the Father; the draw near. The expositors draw the lesson on the confidence: "with confidence" — with confidence (settled assurance); the confidence; the settled; the assurance; the certain; the bold. The witnesses observe the by faith: "by the faith of him" — all received by faith in Christ; the by faith; the in Christ; the received; the faith; the the channel. The expositors note the mediation opens the way: Christ's mediation opens the way to God; the mediation; the opens the way; the Christ's; the to God; the access. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the boldness and access with confidence, the by the faith of him, and the confident access. The verse thus records the believer's confident access in Christ — teaching the free and confident approach to God through Christ, received by faith.
13:
"Wherefore I desire that ye faint not at my tribulations for you, which is your glory." The witnesses note Paul's plea that his sufferings not discourage them. Gill notes Paul was a man of many tribulations and great afflictions, which he suffered not as an evildoer; he was not ashamed of them but gloried and took pleasure in them, having much of God's presence in them, and looking to the glory that should follow. The witnesses note Paul desiring that they not faint at his tribulations endured for them, which are their glory; the sufferings of the apostle turned to the church's honour — that Paul, knowing his imprisonment might dishearten the Gentile believers (as if their cause were shameful or failing), entreats them not to lose heart, for his very afflictions, borne for their sakes in defending and advancing their gospel, are not their shame but their glory, a token of how precious their salvation is; which is your glory. The witnesses note that ye faint not at my tribulations for you. The witnesses note which is your glory.

The witnesses note Paul's plea that his sufferings not dishearten them — his afflictions borne for their sakes are not their shame but their glory.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's plea against discouragement at his sufferings. The lesson the expositors draw is that the apostle's afflictions, borne for the church, are its honour, not its shame. The witnesses note the faint not: "that ye faint not at my tribulations for you" — Paul desires they not faint at his tribulations; the faint not; the tribulations; the for you; the discouraged; the lose heart. The expositors observe Paul's sufferings: Gill notes Paul was a man of many tribulations, gloried in them; the sufferings; the tribulations; the gloried; the not ashamed; the afflictions. The witnesses press the for them: the tribulations were borne for the Gentile believers; the for them; the for their sakes; the the Gentiles; the borne; the their cause. The expositors draw the lesson on the not discouraging: Paul entreats them not to lose heart, as if their cause were shameful or failing; the not discouraging; the lose heart; the shameful; the failing; the entreats. The witnesses observe the their glory: "which is your glory" — his afflictions are not their shame but their glory; the their glory; the not shame; the honour; the token; the precious. The expositors note the precious salvation: his sufferings show how precious their salvation is; the precious salvation; the how precious; the show; the their salvation; the worth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye faint not at my tribulations for you, the which is your glory, and the sufferings turned to honour. The verse thus records Paul's plea against discouragement — teaching that the apostle's afflictions, borne for the church, are its honour, not its shame.
14:
"For this cause I bow my knees unto the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ," The witnesses note Paul's bowing in prayer to the Father. Gill notes Paul prays for the perseverance of the saints, which is the gift of God and his promise, and therefore to be sought for; the gesture he uses, bowing the knees, expresses reverence and humility in prayer. The witnesses note Paul declaring that for this cause he bows his knees to the Father of the Lord Jesus Christ; the reverent posture of earnest intercession — that Paul, resuming the prayer begun in verse 1, turns to fervent intercession for the Ephesians, and marks its earnestness and reverence by the very posture of his body: he bows his knees (kneels) before the Father, the God and Father of the Lord Jesus, in humble, worshipful supplication for their spiritual establishment; I bow my knees unto the Father. The witnesses note for this cause I bow my knees. The witnesses note unto the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note Paul bowing his knees to the Father — the reverent posture of earnest intercession, resuming his great prayer for the Ephesians.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's reverent bowing in prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is the posture of earnest, humble intercession before the Father. The witnesses note the for this cause: "for this cause" — resuming the prayer begun in v.1; the for this cause; the resuming; the v.1; the the prayer; the again. The expositors observe the bowing: "I bow my knees" — Gill notes the gesture expresses reverence and humility; the bowing; the the knees; the kneels; the reverence; the humility. The witnesses press the earnestness: the bowing marks the earnestness and reverence of the prayer; the earnestness; the reverence; the the posture; the fervent; the humble. The expositors draw the lesson on the intercession: Paul turns to fervent intercession for the Ephesians; the intercession; the fervent; the for the Ephesians; the prayer; the supplication. The witnesses observe the to the Father: "unto the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the prayer is to the Father of the Lord Jesus; the to the Father; the of the Lord Jesus; the God and Father; the worshipful; the supplication. The expositors note the humble supplication: the prayer is humble, worshipful supplication for their establishment; the humble supplication; the worshipful; the establishment; the spiritual; the prayer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for this cause I bow my knees, the unto the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, and the reverent intercession. The verse thus records Paul's reverent bowing in prayer — teaching the posture of earnest, humble intercession before the Father.
15:
"Of whom the whole family in heaven and earth is named," The witnesses note that the whole family is named of the Father. Gill notes this may refer to God the Father, who is the Father of the whole family in heaven and earth — the Father of Christ, of spirits, of angels, and of all the saints by adopting grace, whether above or below, all the household of God. The witnesses note Paul observing that of the Father the whole family in heaven and earth is named; the one family of God in heaven and earth — that Paul, having named the Father, marks that from him the entire family of God takes its name and being: the holy angels and the glorified saints above, and the believers still on earth below, are all one household, deriving their sonship and their very name as God's family from the one Father, so that heaven and earth are united in him; the whole family in heaven and earth is named. The witnesses note of whom the whole family. The witnesses note in heaven and earth is named.

The witnesses note the whole family in heaven and earth named of the Father — angels and glorified saints above and believers below all one household, deriving their name from him.

So the witnesses make the verse the one family of God in heaven and earth. The lesson the expositors draw is that the entire family of God, above and below, takes its name and being from the one Father. The witnesses note the of whom: "of whom the whole family" — Gill notes this may refer to the Father, of whom the whole family is named; the of whom; the the Father; the the whole family; the named; the derives. The expositors observe the in heaven and earth: "in heaven and earth is named" — the family in heaven and earth; the in heaven; the and earth; the named; the the household; the one family. The witnesses press the family: the family includes the angels, the glorified saints, and the believers below; the family; the the angels; the glorified saints; the believers below; the household. The expositors draw the lesson on the one household: all are one household, deriving their name from the one Father; the one household; the all; the deriving; the the name; the the Father. The witnesses observe the sonship: they derive their sonship and their very name as God's family from the Father; the sonship; the the name; the God's family; the from the Father; the derived. The expositors note the heaven and earth united: heaven and earth are united in the one Father; the heaven and earth; the united; the in the Father; the one; the household. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of whom the whole family, the in heaven and earth is named, and the one family of God. The verse thus records the one family of God in heaven and earth — teaching that the entire family of God, above and below, takes its name and being from the one Father.
16:
"That he would grant you, according to the riches of his glory, to be strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man;" The witnesses note the first petition — inward strengthening by the Spirit. Gill notes the grant is according to, and out of, that rich, plenteous, and glorious fulness of grace and strength in Christ Jesus. Barnes notes it is according to the glorious abundance of his mercy, out of stores of rich grace that can never be exhausted. The witnesses note Paul praying that God would grant them, according to the riches of his glory, to be strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man; the inward strengthening from God's rich glory — that Paul's first great request is for inward power: that God, out of the boundless riches of his glory (not sparingly, but in proportion to his own infinite fulness), would strengthen the believers with might by his Spirit in the "inner man" (the renewed soul, the hidden person of the heart), making them mighty within for faith, holiness, and endurance; strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man. The witnesses note according to the riches of his glory. The witnesses note strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man.

The witnesses note the first petition — inward strengthening by the Spirit in the inner man, according to the riches of God's glory; might within for faith and holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse the petition for inward strengthening. The lesson the expositors draw is the inward power from God's rich glory, the Spirit making the believer mighty within. The witnesses note the riches: "according to the riches of his glory" — Gill notes out of the rich, glorious fulness of grace; the riches; the of his glory; the the fulness; the boundless; the abundant. The expositors observe the measure: Barnes notes the grant is in proportion to God's infinite fulness, never exhausted; the measure; the proportion; the infinite; the never exhausted; the abundant. The witnesses press the strengthened: "to be strengthened with might by his Spirit" — strengthened with might by the Spirit; the strengthened; the with might; the by the Spirit; the inward power; the mighty. The expositors draw the lesson on the inner man: "in the inner man" — in the inner man (the renewed soul, the hidden person of the heart); the inner man; the renewed soul; the hidden person; the the heart; the within. The witnesses observe the mighty within: the believers are made mighty within for faith, holiness, and endurance; the mighty within; the faith; the holiness; the endurance; the strengthened. The expositors note the from God's glory: the strength flows from the riches of God's glory; the from God's glory; the the riches; the flows; the source; the abundant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the according to the riches of his glory, the strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man, and the inward strengthening. The verse thus records the petition for inward strengthening — teaching the inward power from God's rich glory, the Spirit making the believer mighty within.
17:
"That Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith; that ye, being rooted and grounded in love," The witnesses note the petition for Christ's indwelling and the believer's rooting in love. Gill notes the inhabitant is Christ, who dwells in the highest heavens and in the Father, in whom all fulness dwells, so that those in whose hearts he dwells cannot want any good thing and are in the greatest safety. The witnesses note Paul praying that Christ may dwell in their hearts by faith, they being rooted and grounded in love; the indwelling Christ and the foundation of love — that Paul's prayer rises to its heart: that Christ himself may take up a settled, permanent dwelling in their hearts (received and retained by faith), and that they, like a tree well-rooted and a building well-founded, may be firmly established in love (God's love to them and theirs to him and one another), the soil and foundation from which all spiritual growth and stability spring; Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith. The witnesses note that Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith. The witnesses note being rooted and grounded in love.

The witnesses note the petition for Christ's indwelling by faith and the believer's rooting and grounding in love — the soil and foundation of all spiritual growth.

So the witnesses make the verse the indwelling Christ and the foundation of love. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ takes up a settled dwelling in the heart by faith, and the believer is established in love. The witnesses note the indwelling: "that Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith" — Gill notes Christ the inhabitant, in whom all fulness dwells; the indwelling; the Christ may dwell; the in your hearts; the by faith; the settled. The expositors observe the by faith: Christ is received and retained by faith; the by faith; the received; the retained; the the channel; the faith. The witnesses press the settled dwelling: Christ takes up a settled, permanent dwelling; the settled dwelling; the permanent; the takes up; the abides; the dwells. The expositors draw the lesson on the rooted and grounded: "being rooted and grounded in love" — like a tree well-rooted and a building well-founded in love; the rooted and grounded; the the tree; the the building; the in love; the established. The witnesses observe the foundation of love: love (God's to them and theirs to him and one another) is the soil and foundation; the foundation of love; the God's love; the theirs; the soil; the foundation. The expositors note the growth and stability: from this soil and foundation spring all spiritual growth and stability; the growth and stability; the spring; the the soil; the foundation; the established. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith, the being rooted and grounded in love, and the indwelling and the foundation. The verse thus records the indwelling Christ and the foundation of love — teaching that Christ takes up a settled dwelling in the heart by faith, and the believer is established in love.
18:
"May be able to comprehend with all saints what is the breadth, and length, and depth, and height;" The witnesses note the petition to comprehend the vast dimensions. Gill notes this is the end of their being rooted and grounded in love — that they, together with all the saints interested in it, might have a larger and more comprehensive view. Barnes notes it was his desire that others as well as they might appreciate the wonders of redemption. The witnesses note Paul praying that they may be able to comprehend, with all saints, the breadth, length, depth, and height; the grasp of the immeasurable love and grace of God — that Paul prays the believers (rooted in love) may be enabled, in company with all the saints, to take in the vast dimensions — breadth, length, depth, and height — of God's love in Christ (or of the whole mystery of redemption), a greatness so immense that it can be apprehended only in part, and only by the whole communion of the saints together; the breadth, and length, and depth, and height. The witnesses note may be able to comprehend with all saints. The witnesses note the breadth, and length, and depth, and height.

The witnesses note the petition to comprehend, with all saints, the breadth, length, depth, and height — the immeasurable dimensions of God's love in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the petition to grasp the vast dimensions. The lesson the expositors draw is the grasp of the immeasurable love and grace of God, apprehended only with all the saints. The witnesses note the comprehend: "may be able to comprehend with all saints" — Gill notes this is the end of being rooted in love; the comprehend; the with all saints; the the end; the grasp; the take in. The expositors observe the with all saints: the comprehension is with all the saints together; the with all saints; the together; the the communion; the all; the shared. The witnesses press the dimensions: "what is the breadth, and length, and depth, and height" — the breadth, length, depth, and height; the dimensions; the breadth; the length; the depth and height; the vast. The expositors draw the lesson on the immeasurable: the dimensions are of God's love in Christ (or the whole mystery), immeasurably vast; the immeasurable; the God's love; the the mystery; the vast; the greatness. The witnesses observe the apprehended in part: the greatness can be apprehended only in part; the in part; the only; the apprehended; the vast; the immense. The expositors note the only with the saints: it is grasped only by the whole communion of the saints together; the only with the saints; the the communion; the together; the all; the shared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the may be able to comprehend with all saints, the the breadth and length and depth and height, and the vast dimensions. The verse thus records the petition to grasp the vast dimensions — teaching the grasp of the immeasurable love and grace of God, apprehended only with all the saints.
19:
"And to know the love of Christ, which passeth knowledge, that ye might be filled with all the fulness of God." The witnesses note the petition to know the love of Christ and be filled with God's fulness. Gill notes the love of Christ to his church is special and peculiar, free and sovereign, as early as the Father's love, durable and unchangeable, the greatest love ever heard of, matchless and unparalleled, exceeding strong and wonderful. The witnesses note Paul praying that they might know the love of Christ which passes knowledge, that they might be filled with all the fulness of God; the knowledge of the boundless love and the fulness of God — that Paul's prayer reaches its height in two immense petitions: that they may "know" (by experience and in measure) the love of Christ which yet surpasses all knowledge (so vast it can never be fully comprehended), and that they may be "filled with all the fulness of God" — filled, according to their capacity, with the graces and likeness of God, as full of God as a creature can be; the love of Christ, which passeth knowledge. The witnesses note to know the love of Christ, which passeth knowledge. The witnesses note filled with all the fulness of God.

The witnesses note the petition to know the love of Christ that passes knowledge, and to be filled with all the fulness of God — the height of Paul's prayer.

So the witnesses make the verse the knowledge of Christ's love and the fulness of God. The lesson the expositors draw is the two immense petitions: to know the love that surpasses knowledge and to be filled with God's fulness. The witnesses note the love of Christ: "to know the love of Christ, which passeth knowledge" — Gill notes Christ's love is special, free, sovereign, the greatest ever heard of; the love of Christ; the special; the free; the sovereign; the greatest. The expositors observe the passeth knowledge: the love passes (surpasses) all knowledge; the passeth knowledge; the surpasses; the never fully known; the vast; the boundless. The witnesses press the to know: "to know the love" — to know it by experience and in measure; the to know; the by experience; the in measure; the apprehend; the taste. The expositors draw the lesson on the paradox: they are to know what passes knowledge — knowing in part what can never be fully comprehended; the paradox; the know; the passes knowledge; the in part; the never fully. The witnesses observe the filled with the fulness: "that ye might be filled with all the fulness of God" — filled with all the fulness of God; the filled; the the fulness; the of God; the according to capacity; the full. The expositors note the as full as a creature can be: filled, according to their capacity, with the graces and likeness of God, as full of God as a creature can be; the as full as a creature; the capacity; the graces; the likeness; the filled. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to know the love of Christ which passeth knowledge, the filled with all the fulness of God, and the height of the prayer. The verse thus records the knowledge of Christ's love and the fulness of God — teaching the two immense petitions: to know the love that surpasses knowledge and to be filled with God's fulness.
20:
"Now unto him that is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to the power that worketh in us," The witnesses note the doxology to God's surpassing power. Gill notes this conclusion of the prayer celebrates the power of God, a perfection essential to him and very extensive — reaching to all he wills, and to more than he has willed, to all things past, present, and future, and to things impossible to nature. The witnesses note Paul ascribing praise to God, who is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to the power that works in believers; the God whose power exceeds all asking and thought — that Paul, his great prayer ended, breaks into praise of the God whose ability infinitely surpasses even the largest petitions and highest imaginings of his people, piling word on word ("exceeding abundantly above all") to reach after the immeasurable, and grounding the assurance of it in the very power already at work within them by the Spirit; able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think. The witnesses note able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think. The witnesses note according to the power that worketh in us.

The witnesses note the doxology — God able to do exceeding abundantly above all we ask or think, according to the power already at work in believers.

So the witnesses make the verse the doxology to God's surpassing power. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's ability infinitely surpasses the largest petitions, grounded in the power at work within. The witnesses note the able: "him that is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think" — Gill notes the power of God, reaching beyond all he wills; the able; the exceeding abundantly; the above all; the ask or think; the power. The expositors observe the surpassing: God's ability surpasses even the largest petitions and highest imaginings; the surpassing; the largest petitions; the highest imaginings; the above all; the immeasurable. The witnesses press the piling of words: Paul piles word on word ("exceeding abundantly above all") to reach after the immeasurable; the piling of words; the exceeding abundantly; the above all; the reach; the immeasurable. The expositors draw the lesson on the power within: "according to the power that worketh in us" — grounded in the power already at work within; the power within; the at work; the in us; the the Spirit; the grounded. The witnesses observe the assurance: the assurance of it rests on the power already working by the Spirit; the assurance; the rests; the the power; the working; the the Spirit. The expositors note the praise of God: the prayer ends in praise of the God of surpassing power; the praise; the of God; the surpassing power; the doxology; the worship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think, the according to the power that worketh in us, and the surpassing power. The verse thus records the doxology to God's surpassing power — teaching that God's ability infinitely surpasses the largest petitions, grounded in the power at work within.
21:
"Unto him be glory in the church by Christ Jesus throughout all ages, world without end. Amen." The witnesses note the ascription of everlasting glory to God in the church. Gill notes this doxology gives glory to God on account of his perfections, the works of creation and providence, and especially for spiritual mercies; glory rendered in the church, the proper place, and by Christ Jesus, the only Mediator. The witnesses note Paul ascribing glory to God in the church by Christ Jesus throughout all ages, world without end, sealed with "Amen"; the eternal glory of God in the church through Christ — that Paul ends the doxology by giving all glory to God, and naming the place and the means: glory rendered to him "in the church" (the redeemed company being the great theatre and instrument of his praise) and "by Christ Jesus" (the one Mediator through whom alone acceptable praise ascends), and that without end, through all generations and into eternity; glory in the church by Christ Jesus throughout all ages. The witnesses note unto him be glory in the church by Christ Jesus. The witnesses note throughout all ages, world without end. Amen.

The witnesses note the ascription of everlasting glory to God in the church by Christ Jesus, through all ages — the redeemed the theatre of his praise, Christ the Mediator.

So the witnesses make the verse the eternal glory of God in the church through Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the rendering of all glory to God in the church and by Christ, world without end. The witnesses note the glory: "unto him be glory" — Gill notes glory rendered to God for his perfections and mercies; the glory; the unto him; the perfections; the mercies; the ascribed. The expositors observe the in the church: "in the church" — glory rendered in the church, the proper place; the in the church; the proper place; the the redeemed; the theatre; the instrument. The witnesses press the by Christ: "by Christ Jesus" — by Christ Jesus, the only Mediator; the by Christ; the the Mediator; the the only; the through him; the acceptable. The expositors draw the lesson on the place and means: the glory is rendered in the church (the theatre of praise) and by Christ (the means of acceptable praise); the place and means; the in the church; the by Christ; the theatre; the Mediator. The witnesses observe the without end: "throughout all ages, world without end" — through all generations and into eternity; the without end; the all ages; the world without end; the eternal; the everlasting. The expositors note the Amen: the doxology is sealed with "Amen"; the Amen; the sealed; the so be it; the the doxology; the close. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto him be glory in the church by Christ Jesus, the throughout all ages world without end Amen, and the eternal glory. The verse thus records the eternal glory of God in the church through Christ — teaching the rendering of all glory to God in the church and by Christ, world without end.
